content.question: Would a structural reform postponing the 6/8-year track selection (e.g., moving it from ages 10–12 to age 14) produce measurable improvements in equity, and at what implementation cost in terms of school closures, teacher displacement, and transition-period overcrowding?
content.reduction_path: Before a full legislative reversal, pilot a delayed-selection intervention in a subset of Hungarian districts (stratified by urban/rural, low/high prior segregation) for 3–5 years. Measure PISA outcomes, between-school variance in socioeconomic composition, and teacher labor market disruption. Parallel qualitative study of parent/student experience and political feasibility. Use Polish 1999 and 2017 cases as counterfactuals to model implementation risks.
